items.title: Burn depth
items.contentHtml:
Classification by depth

  • 1st degree: affects only epidermis; erythema, pain, without flictenae; heals in 3-5 days without scar.
  • 2° superficial: epidermis and papillary dermis; flictenas, moist pink base, very painful; reepithelializes in 10-14 days.
  • 2° deep: reticular dermis; broken flictenae, pale red or mottled base, hypoalgesia; requires >21 days or excision.
  • 3rd degree: full thickness (epidermis, dermis, annexes); cardboardy, white or charred appearance, painless; does not heal spontaneously.
  • 4th degree: involves fascia, muscle and bone; always requires surgical debridement.

Trap examination: the superficial 2° hurts; the 2° deep hurts less. The presence of flictenae does not distinguish between superficial and deep.

items.foco: Rule of 9 for burned body surface
items.title: Rule of 9
items.contentHtml:
Rule of 9 (adult)

  • Head and neck: 9%.
  • Anterior trunk: 18%; posterior trunk: 18%.
  • Right upper limb: 9%; left: 9%.
  • Right lower limb: 18%; left: 18%.
  • Perineum: 1%.

Modifications: in children, head 18% and each leg 13.5%. The palm of the patient's hand (without fingers) is equivalent to 0.5% and with fingers to 1% (useful for irregular areas).

Trap: do not add 1st degree burns in the calculation of burned body surface area (SCQ); only 2nd and 3rd.

items.title: Parkland Formula
items.contentHtml:
Parkland Formula

Indication: 2nd and 3rd degree burns with SCQ >20% in adults and >10% in children. Formula: 4 mL/kg/%SCQ of lactated Ringer's in the first 24 hours from the time of the burn (not from admission).

  • First half in the first 8 hours.
  • Second half in the following 16 hours.
  • In children, add maintenance with 5% dextrose in lactated Ringer's.

Trap: the formula is only a guide; adjust according to hourly diuresis (0.5-1 mL/kg/h in adults, 1-1.5 mL/kg/h in children). Do not use colloids in the first 24 hours (except in burns >40% or refractory shock).

items.title: ABCDE in burns
items.contentHtml:
Initial management ABCDE

  • A (airway): evaluate stridor, hoarseness, carbonaceous cough, facial burns; early intubation if inhalation burn or progressive edema is suspected.
  • B (breathing): administer 100% O2 if there is inhalation; monitor expired CO2.
  • C (circulation): two peripheral venous accesses (avoid burned areas if possible); begin replacement with Ringer's lactate according to Parkland.
  • D (disability): evaluate Glasgow; suspect carbon monoxide or cyanide poisoning.
  • E (exposure): remove clothing, evaluate SCQ, cover with sterile sheets; maintain temperature.

Trap: do not delay intubation waiting for confirmation; airway edema can make it impossible in minutes.

items.title: Gravity and reference
items.contentHtml:
Severity scale (American Burn Association)

  • Mild: 2° <10% in adults or <5% in children; 3rd <2%.
  • Moderate: 2nd 10-20% in adults or 5-10% in children; 3rd <10%.
  • Severe: 2nd >20% in adults or >10% in children; 3rd >10%; burns of hands, feet, face, perineum, joints; electrical; inhalation; polytrauma; extremes of age.

Reference criteria to burn unit: full thickness burns >10% SCQ, 2nd and 3rd in special areas, electrical, chemical, inhalation, significant comorbidities, children.

items.foco: Electrical burn: risk of arrhythmias and rhabdomyolysis
items.title: electrical burn
items.contentHtml:
Electrical burn

Main risk: occult injury in deep muscle, cardiac arrhythmias and rhabdomyolysis. Mechanism: heat damage, electroporation and tissue necrosis.

  • Evaluate ECG on admission: look for ventricular fibrillation, tachyarrhythmias, blocks.
  • Cardiac monitoring for 24 hours if there was loss of consciousness or initial arrhythmia.
  • Measure total CK and myoglobinuria; maintain diuresis >1 mL/kg/h with lactated Ringer's and sodium bicarbonate if pH <7.2.
  • Early escharotomy/fasciotomy due to compartment syndrome.

Trap: the external lesion does not correlate with the internal one; there may be extensive muscle necrosis with intact skin.

items.foco: Chemical burn: copious irrigation and neutralization
items.title: chemical burn
items.contentHtml:
Chemical burn

First measure: copious irrigation with water or saline solution for 20-30 minutes (at least 10 liters). Remove contaminated clothing and dry dust before irrigating.

  • DO NOT neutralize with acids or bases; the exothermic reaction worsens the injury.
  • Exceptions: phenol (irrigate with polyethylene glycol), hydrofluoric acid (apply calcium gluconate topically or intralesionally), and white phosphorus (cover with copper sulfate to identify particles).
  • Eye burns: continuous irrigation with Ringer's solution or water, and refer to urgent ophthalmology.

Trap: do not use neutralizers; Irrigation is the cornerstone of treatment.

items.foco: Inhalation burn: diagnosis by bronchoscopy and carboxyhemoglobin
items.title: inhalation burn
items.contentHtml:
Inhalation burn

Diagnosis: clinical suspicion (confinement, loss of consciousness, carbonaceous sputum, stridor). Gold standard: bronchoscopy (edema, erythema, carbonaceous, necrosis). Carboxyhemoglobin (COHb) >10% confirms carbon monoxide exposure.

  • Treatment: 100% O2 until COHb <5% or <10% in non-smokers.
  • Consider hyperbaric chamber if COHb >25%, loss of consciousness, metabolic acidosis or pregnancy.
  • Early intubation if glottic edema or ventilatory compromise.

Trap: SaO2 by pulse oximetry may be normal (COHb is measured with co-oximetry).

items.title: Escharotomy and fasciotomy
items.contentHtml:
Indications for escharotomy and fasciotomy

Escharotomy: 3rd degree circumferential burns on the chest (ventilatory restriction) or extremities (compartment syndrome). It is performed at the level of the eschar, without general anesthesia (the eschar is insensitive).

  • Fasciotomy: in electrical or compression burns, when there is muscle compromise and intracompartmental pressure >30 mmHg.
  • Signs of compartment syndrome: disproportionate pain, paresthesias, paresis, absent (delayed) distal pulse.
  • Technique: longitudinal incisions in the midline of the extremity, releasing eschar and fascia.

Trap: the absence of a pulse is a late sign; Do not wait for it to disappear before intervening.

items.foco: Infection prevention: silver sulfadiazine and mupirocin
items.title: Infection prevention
items.contentHtml:
Prevention of topical infection

  • Silver sulfadiazine 1% (cream): first line in 2° and 3° burns; covers Gram+ and Gram-; apply a 2 mm layer every 12-24 hours. Contraindicated in pregnancy and newborns (risk of kernicterus).
  • Mupirocin (ointment): indicated for facial burns and areas with S. aureusresistant; do not use on large surfaces due to potential nephrotoxicity.
  • Alternatives: mafenide acetate (penetrates eschar, useful in cartilage; painful and metabolic acidosis) and 0.5% silver nitrate (solution, less penetration).

Trap: do not use silver sulfadiazine in deep burns with tendon exposure or bone (delays epithelialization).

items.foco: Criteria for admission to the burn unit
items.title: Admission to burn unit
items.contentHtml:
Criteria for admission to the burn unit (ABA)

  • 2nd degree burns >10% SCQ in patients <10 years or >50 years old.
  • 2nd degree burns >20% SCQ in other adults.
  • 3rd degree burns >5% SCQ at any age.
  • Burns in face, hands, feet, perineum, genitals, major joints.
  • Electrical burns (including lightning).
  • Chemical burns with systemic risk.
  • Associated inhalation burns.
  • Patients with comorbidities (diabetes, heart disease, immunosuppression) or polytrauma.

Trap: the burn of the perineum is always a criterion for admission to the burn unit, regardless of the SCQ.

items.foco: Systemic complications: sepsis, renal failure and hypermetabolic response
items.title: Systemic complications
items.contentHtml:
Systemic complications

  • Sepsis: main cause of late death. Criteria: temperature >39°C or <36.5°C, tachycardia, tachypnea, leukocytosis/leukopenia, hypotension. Eschar cultures and blood cultures. Empirical antibiotic treatment with Gram+ and Gram- coverage (piperacillin-tazobactam + vancomycin).
  • Acute kidney failure: due to hypovolemia, rhabdomyolysis or sepsis. Prevention with adequate water replacement and maintaining diuresis. Dialysis if refractory.
  • Hypermetabolic response: increase in energy expenditure up to 2 times basal; requires early enteral nutritional support (high protein), strict glycemic control (glucose <180 mg/dL) and avoidance of hypothermia.

Trap: hyperglycemia increases the risk of infection and graft failure.
